title: Resident Memory T Cells in the Atherosclerotic Lesion Associate With Reduced Macrophage Content and Increased Lesion Stability.
author_string: de Jong, Maaike J M, Depuydt, Marie A C, Schaftenaar, Frank H, Liu, Kun, Maters, David et al.
abstract: Tissue resident memory T (T<sub>RM</sub>) cells are a T-cell subset that resides at the site of prior antigen recognition to protect the body against reoccurring encounters. Besides their protective function, T<sub>RM</sub> cells have also been implicated in inflammatory disorders. T<sub>RM</sub> cells are characterized by the expression of CD69 and transcription factors Hobit (homolog of Blimp-1 [B lymphocyte-induced maturation protein 1] in T cells) and Blimp-1. As the majority of T cells in the arterial intima expresses CD69, T<sub>RM</sub> cells may contribute to the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis as well. Here, we aimed to assess the presence and potential role of T<sub>RM</sub> cells in atherosclerosis. To identify T<sub>RM</sub> cells in human atherosclerotic lesions, a single-cell RNA-sequencing data set was interrogated, and T-cell phenotypes were compared with that of integrated predefined T<sub>RM</sub> cells. The presence and phenotype of T<sub>RM</sub> in atherosclerotic lesions was corroborated using a mouse model that enabled tracking of Hobit-expressing T<sub>RM</sub> cells. To explore the function of T<sub>RM</sub> cells during atherogenesis, RAG1<sup>-/-</sup> (recombination activating gene 1 deficient) LDLr<sup>-/-</sup> (low-density lipoprotein receptor knockout) mice received a bone marrow transplant from Hobit<sup>KO/CRE</sup>Blimp-1<sup>flox/flox</sup> mice, which exhibit abrogated T<sub>RM</sub> cell formation, whereafter the mice were fed a Western-type diet for 10 weeks. Human atherosclerotic lesions contained T cells that exhibited a T<sub>RM</sub> cell-associated gene signature. Moreover, a fraction of these T cells clustered together with predefined T<sub>RM</sub> cells upon integration. The presence of Hobit-expressing T<sub>RM</sub> cells in the atherosclerotic lesion was confirmed in mice. These lesion-derived T<sub>RM</sub> cells were characterized by the expression of CD69 and CD49α. Moreover, we demonstrated that this small T-cell subset significantly affects lesion composition, by reducing the amount of intralesional macrophages and increasing collagen content. T<sub>RM</sub> cells, characterized by the expression of CD69 and CD49α, constitute a minor population in atherosclerotic lesions and are associated with increased lesion stability in a Hobit and Blimp-1 knockout mouse model.
